7.1 Binding a Specific Target: Antibody Structure and Function

Immunoglobulin Structure and Function (1 of 3)
The immune response is the body’s primary defense against invaders (e.g., pathogens causing infection) and cancer
The immune response system is able to distinguish between “self” and “foreign”
Two types of immune response: innate and adaptive
The adaptive immune response includes humoral and cellular components

Immunoglobulin Structure and Function (2 of 3)
During the humoral response, each B lymphocyte synthesizes an antibody, or immunoglobulin (I  g  G), to a particular antigen
B cells are stimulated to proliferate and secrete the antibody, which binds the antigen, marking it for destruction by macrophages
During cellular response, T lymphocytes, bearing antibody-like structures on their surface, recognize and kill infected or cancerous cells

Immunoglobulin Structure and Function (3 of 3)
Antibodies recognize specific epitopes on antigens
Figure 7.1 Antigenic determinants.
Myoglobin with three different antigenic segments shown in cyan, purple, and orange

Antibodies recognize specific epitopes on antigens
Figure 7.1 Antigenic determinants
Representations of myoglobin (green) bound to antibodies (blue and white). The binding of two epitopes by each antibody can form a crosslinked network which precipitates as an aggregate
7.2 The Adaptive Immune Response

Developmental Paths for Stimulated B Lymphocytes (1 of 2)
An immature B lymphocyte displays on its surface an antibody to a particular antigen
Initial exposure to that antigen stimulates the B cell to proliferate and form effector cells, which secrete a soluble form of the antibody, and memory cells, which mount a larger response upon subsequent exposure to the antigen
This is the basis for the protective effects of vaccines

Developmental Paths for Stimulated B Lymphocytes (2 of 2)
Figure 7.2 Two developmental paths for stimulated B lymphocytes.
7.3 The Structure of Antibodies

Five Classes of Immunoglobulins (1 of 3)
Table 7.1 The five classes of immunoglobulins

Five Classes of Immunoglobulins (2 of 3)
Schematic model of an I g G antibody
Figure 7.3 Schematic models of an I g G antibody molecule and an
fragment.
An IgG antibody is made of two identical heavy chains (with carbohydrates/C H O attached) and two identical light chains, held together by disulfide bridges. Each chain contains constant (C) and variable (V) domains. V domains confer specificity to antigenic determinants. An IgG antibody can be proteolytically separated into two

Five Classes of Immunoglobulins (3 of 3)
Crystal structure of an I g G antibody
Figure 7.4 The crystal structure of an I g G molecule from mouse.
Light and heavy chains with antigen-binding sites (two per antibody) in high resolution crystal structure
7.4 Antibody-Antigen Interactions

The Antigen-Binding Site (1 of 2)
Figure 7.6 Antigen binding by an
fragment.
The antigen-binding site is hypervariable and comprises the complementarity-determining regions of the antibody amino acid sequence. These hypervariable sites are also called “CDR loops” and are located at the ends of the antibody variable regions

The Antigen-Binding Site (2 of 2)
Figure 7.6 Antigen binding by an
fragment.

Contact Between Antigen and Antibody Surface
Figure 7.6 Antigen binding by an
fragment.
The antibody light chain is shown in green, the heavy chain is in yellow, and the antigen is in purple. The surfaces of the antibody and antigen-binding site fit together with a high degree of shape and charge complementarity

7.6 The Challenge of Developing an A I D S Vaccine

The Human Immunodeficiency Virus and the Immune Response (1 of 2)
An effective vaccine against the Human Immunodeficiency Virus (H I V) has yet not been developed
H I V attacks immune response cells, particularly T-Helper
cells that activate B cells to produce neutralizing
antibodies
H I V mutates quickly and hence changes the structure of antigenic determinants frequently

The Human Immunodeficiency Virus and the Immune Response (2 of 2)
Figure 7.10 The human immunodeficiency virus, or H I V.
Electron micrograph of H I V particles
Budding of H I V particles on the surface of human lymphocytes

Models for Allosteric Transitions in Hemoglobin (1 of 2)
Figure 7.20 Two classical models for the cooperative ligand binding by hemoglobin.
K N F (Koshland/Nemethy/Filmer; sequential) model: Binding
at one subunit changes conformation at that subunit, facilitating transition at adjacent subunits in the same molecule; mixed
tetramers with low- and high-
binding affinity subunits are allowed

Models for Allosteric Transitions in Hemoglobin (2 of 2)
Figure 7.20 Two classical models for the cooperative ligand binding by hemoglobin.
M W C (Monod/Wyman/Changeux; symmetry) model: Hb exists in two
states: T (tense; lower
binding affinity) and R (relaxed);
binding perturbs the
equilibrium toward the R state, state, and
release favors the T state; mixed tetramers are not allowed in this
model

7.11 Allosteric Effectors of Hemoglobin Promote Efficient Oxygen Delivery to Tissues

Binding of Allosteric Effectors to Hemoglobin Modulates O2 Binding and Release (1 of 3)
Allosteric effectors bind their target proteins and promote a conformational change that modulates the functional properties of these proteins
e.g., increase/decrease
binding affinity in hemoglobin,
or increase/decrease the activity of an enzyme
Homotropic allosteric effectors bind at the active site, whereas heterotropic effectors bind at other sites on the protein

Binding of Allosteric Effectors to Hemoglobin Modulates O2 Binding and Release (2 of 3)

7.13 Mechanisms of Protein Mutation

Types of Mutation (1 of 2)
Most mutations occur in somatic cells and are not carried forth to the next generation
Evolutionary relationships are defined by considering rates of mutation in genomes and differences in amino acid sequences for homologous proteins
For example, there are 25 amino acid differences between human and sperm whale myoglobins, suggesting that the two species diverged about 100 million years ago

Types of Mutation (2 of 2)
Figure 7.32 Mutation types.

Evolutionary Tree Based on Globin Sequences (1 of 2)
Figure 7.34 Evolution of the globin genes.

Evolutionary Tree Based on Globin Sequences (2 of 2)
Each branch in the evolutionary tree involves a gene duplication; the more recent, the more closely related
The lamprey is the most primitive form to contain hemoglobin (dimeric)
The overall globin fold and surrounding heme ring are invariant, as are the two critical His residues and most
residues involving contacts between
and

Distributions of Mutations in Human Hemoglobin
Pathological mutations (substitutions) are indicated by red dots, while nonpathological mutations are indicated by blue dots
Figure 7.35 Distribution of mutations in human hemoglobins.

Missense Mutations in Hemoglobins
Table 7.2 Selected list of missense mutations in human hemoglobins

Sickle-Cell Disease (1 of 2)
Figure 7.37 Erythrocytes in sickle-cell disease.
Abnormal (sickle-celled) erythrocytes block circulation in capillaries and lyse due to their fragility, causing anemia. Heterozygotes with half mutant and half normal H b are asymptomatic except when oxygen-stressed

Sickle-Cell Disease (2 of 2)
Figure 7.38 Sickle-cell hemoglobin.
7.15 Protein Function Requiring Large Conformational Changes: Muscle Contraction

Functioning in Large Protein Complexes
The span of functional protein units reaches from monomers to multi-subunit structures
Motor proteins carrying out the mechanical work of motion within cells, tissues or entire organisms are large complexes with many single polypeptides and other components involved for proper function
One set of motor proteins used in muscles and some contractile systems is actin and myosin
Actin and myosin convert the energy of A T P hydrolysis into mechanical work

Figure 7.39 G-actin.
A T P binding by globular actin (G-actin) leads to polymerization and the formation of filamentous actin (F-actin)

Myosin II Structure, Entire Molecule and S1 Fragments
Functional myosin molecule is composed of six polypeptide chains: two heavy chains, two essential light chains, and two regulatory light chains. Proteolyic cleavage can digest  myosin into two S1 fragments (with head domains) and a coiled-coil (or tail) domain

7.19 Tools of Biochemistry

Immunological Methods (1 of 2)
Indirect enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (E L I S A)
Figure 7A.1 The indirect E L I S A assay.

Immunological Methods (2 of 2)
Western blotting
is also called immunoblotting
is used to identify immunoreactive proteins in a complex mixture
This protein mixture is separated by electrophoresis and transferred to a membrane filter
Subsequently, the transferred proteins are treated with an antibody raised against specific targets
Interactions with this antibody are then detected

Western Blotting
The top panel shows a 2D gel of the total protein from tobacco leaf.
The bottom panel shows the same gel probed with an antibody specific for proteins containing phosphothreonine residues
Figure 7A.2 Western blotting.

Chapter 7 Summary (1 of 3)
The innate and adaptive immune response are the main body defenses against “foreign” invaders
Antibodies or immunoglobulins are humoral components of the adaptive immune response
Antibodies bind specific antigens that are secreted by B cells
Each antibody is composed of two heavy and two light chains, and has two identical antigen-binding sites with complementarity-determining regions (C D R)
E L I S A and Western Blotting are immunological techniques to detect immunoreactive proteins (antigens) in mixtures of cellular extract

Chapter 7 Summary (2 of 3)
Hemoglobin and myoglobin are heme-containing oxygen-binding proteins that are used by vertebrates for oxygen transport and storage
Hemoglobin undergoes a transition between the T (tense) and R (relaxed) state, which is influenced by various allosteric effectors such
(positive homotropic),
and 2,3-bisphosphoglycerate (negative heterotropic)
Allosteric effectors increase binding or release of oxygen from   hemoglobin

Chapter 7 Summary (3 of 3)
Various hemoglobin mutants are found in human populations some are deleterious
A number of multi-subunit protein complexes such as the actin-myosin contractile system are able to convert the free energy of A T P hydrolysis into mechanical work
Coordinated (and regulated) molecular motion of actin-myosin protein complexes result in muscle contraction on the macroscopic level
